title: LLMStructBench: Benchmarking Large Language Model Structured Data Extraction
authors: Sönke Tenckhoff, Mario Koddenbrock, Erik Rodner (KI-Werkstatt / FB2, University of Applied Sciences Berlin (HTW Berlin))
venue: arXiv preprint (cs.CL, cross-listed cs.LG); no journal/conference venue listed; v1 submitted 16 Feb 2026 (paper header, lines 2-10)
claim: LLMStructBench introduces a 995-instance, five-use-case benchmark of natural-language-message-to-JSON extraction, paired with a JSON schema and a graded metric (F1_micro token/value accuracy plus DOC_micro document-level validity) that separates structural validity from semantic correctness. Across 22 open-weight models (0.6B-70B, five families) plus GPT-4o as a closed reference, and five prompting strategies that vary API-level JSON-mode enforcement vs. in-prompt schema/example guidance, the paper concludes that choosing the right prompting strategy matters more than model scale for schema-compliant, semantically correct JSON generation (Abstract; Sec. VII Conclusion).
novelty: The authors position their contribution as: (1) an open, manually-verified 995-sample dataset spanning flat-to-deeply-nested JSON schemas across five realistic email/business use cases (Sec. III, Table I); (2) new complementary metrics -- F1_micro (a fuzzy, coercion-aware, Levenshtein-weighted micro-F1 over key/value classification, split 25/75 between key-presence and value-correctness) and DOC_micro (document-level full-correctness/validity share) combined into one composite score (Sec. IV, IV-A, IV-B); (3) a systematic 22-model x 5-prompting-strategy comparison isolating prompting-strategy effects from model-scale effects (Sec. V, VI). They explicitly differentiate from JSONSchemaBench [Geng et al.] (schema-constrained decoding engine compliance only, no semantic value scoring), StructText [Kashyap et al.] (synthetic table-to-text narratives vs. their naturally-occurring-style messages), a GeoJSON/spatial benchmark [Li et al.] (domain-specific), and 'Generate & Organize' / schema-driven-prompting papers [Li et al.; Pradhan et al.] (they extend the schema-driven-prompting idea to a broader open-model pool and analyze resulting error-type shifts) (Sec. II Related Work).

protocol.description: Each test instance is evaluated in one forward call per model per prompting strategy: the model receives a natural-language message, a representative example (text, reference JSON) pair, and the target JSON schema, and is instructed to return a single JSON object matching the schema in one shot -- there is no multi-turn interaction, no iterative repair loop, and no feedback between attempts. This is stated directly: 'Each model receives a natural-language message, a representative example pair (text and reference JSON), and the corresponding JSON schema that defines the required structure. It is instructed to return a single JSON object adhering to this schema' (Sec. III, 'Benchmark setup', also Fig. 1 caption). The five prompting configurations (Table II) vary only how schema/format guidance is delivered (API 'format' parameter set to "json" string vs. full JSON-Schema object; presence/absence of schema+example object in the system/user prompt) -- they are not multi-turn strategies, each is still a single generation call per test case.
protocol.observation_content: Per call the model sees: (a) the natural-language message (the only free-text input) containing all required information; (b) one example input-output pair (representative text + reference JSON) baked into the prompt -- i.e., one-shot demonstration, not few-shot with multiple examples; (c) the JSON schema defining required keys, types, and nesting; and, depending on strategy (Table II), the API-level 'format' request parameter set to either the literal string "json" or the full JSON-Schema object. The paper does not reproduce the literal prompt template text verbatim beyond Table II's strategy descriptions and Fig. 1's schematic caption; the exact wording of system/user prompt strings is not included in the extracted text (Sec. III 'Benchmark setup'; Sec. V-B; Table II).

representations.summary: The paper does not vary the *serialization convention* of the output (there is no house-style/canonicalization axis analogous to c11's key-order/quoting/number-format rules) -- output is always 'a single JSON object adhering to schema', scored by structural/semantic diff rather than byte-exact string match. What it does vary is (1) structural complexity/depth of the target JSON schema across five use cases (Table I: Support tickets depth 2, Sick leave depth 3, Project extension depth 3, Conference registration depth 3, Loan request depth 4 with array-of-objects) and (2) how JSON-formatting is requested from the model across five prompting strategies (J, P, PJ, J+, PJ+; Table II).
representations.effect_on_accuracy: Prompting-strategy effect is large and non-monotonic with structural depth: aggregated across all models, strategy P produced the most fully-correct outputs (7,791) but also many failures (1,597, second-highest), while PJ+ produced nearly as many correct outputs (7,646) with almost no failures (18) (Table III). Strategy ranking by the paper's weighted score: P wins for 11 models, PJ+ for 8, PJ for 3, J and J+ for 0 (Table IV). Schema depth/complexity effect: deeper/nested schemas (e.g., Loan request, depth 4, array-of-objects) were reported as harder to generate synthetic data for at all -- 'We also experimented with use cases and schema that involved more nested JSON-objects, however, this did not result in even medium quality generated examples and have been therefore skipped' (Sec. III-C).

models.name: Qwen3 (0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 32B)
models.key_results: Table V leaderboard (P strategy): Qwen3-14B F1_micro 0.95/DOC_micro 0.44/Score 0.69; Qwen3-1.7B 0.94/0.40/0.67; Qwen3-32B 0.94/0.39/0.67; Qwen3-4B 0.93/0.37/0.65; Qwen3-8B 0.93/0.36/0.65; Qwen3-0.6B 0.92/0.30/0.61. Table VIII: Qwen3-0.6B best strategy PJ+ (score 0.61, DOC_micro improves under PJ+); Qwen3-1.7B best under P (0.67, 'Mid-table despite small size'); Qwen3 4-32B best under P (0.65-0.69, '32B no better than smaller peers').

models.name: Phi family (Phi3-3.8b, Phi3-14b, Phi3.5-3.8b, Phi4-mini-3.8b, Phi4-14b)
models.key_results: Table V: Phi3-14B 0.94/0.38/0.66; Phi4-mini-3.8B 0.95/0.38/0.66; Phi4-14B 0.94/0.38/0.66; Phi3.5-3.8B 0.95/0.03/0.49; Phi3-3.8B 0.95/0.01/0.48 (worst DOC_micro of any model under P, and lowest overall score). Detailed case study in Sec. VI-A/Fig. 2-3: under PJ+, Phi3-3.8B produced 298 perfect JSON objects with 130 errors and zero failures, described as the best strategy for this family. Table VIII: Phi3/3.5-3.8B best under PJ+ (0.48-0.49, 'P poor; PJ+ mitigates structural failures'); Phi3/4-14B best under P (0.66).

models.name: Gemma3 (1B, 12B, 27B)
models.key_results: Table V (P strategy): Gemma3-27B is joint top model, F1_micro 0.96/DOC_micro 0.52/Score 0.74 (tied with GPT-4o); Gemma3-12B 0.95/0.49/0.72 ('punches above its weight, landing in the top three and even eclipsing several 70B models', Sec. VI-C); Gemma3-1B 0.89/0.10/0.49 (only 98 of 955/995 flawless results per Fig. 4/Fig. 5 discussion, Sec. VI-B). Table VIII: Gemma3-1B best under PJ+ (DOC_micro +0.2 improvement); Gemma3-12B/27B best under P.

models.name: Deepseek-R1 (1.5B, 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, 70B)
models.key_results: Table V (P): Deepseek-R1-70B 0.95/0.45/0.70; -14B 0.95/0.43/0.69; -7B 0.94/0.39/0.67; -8B 0.94/0.38/0.66; -32B 0.94/0.33/0.63 ('lags behind smaller peers'); -1.5B 0.90/0.16/0.53. Table VI: MK errors drop from 36.8% (1.5B, P) to near 0% by 8B+; WV errors dominate throughout, 76.7% (1.5B, P) rising to 97-98% (larger models, P) and reaching 99.7-100% under PJ+. Sec. VI-C: WV count for the 1.5B->70B P-strategy sweep starts at 389, peaks at 632 (32B), settles at 501 (70B); N_err drops 790->605 (-23%) for 1.5B when switching P->PJ+; PJ+ leaves 'no run unparseable' for this family.

models.name: LLaMA3 (Llama3.1-70B, Llama3.3-70B)
models.key_results: Table V (P): Llama3.1-70B 0.95/0.51/0.73; Llama3.3-70B 0.95/0.45/0.70. Table VIII: both best under P, score range 0.70-0.73, 'DOC_micro variance increases with size', dominant error WV.

models.name: GPT-4o (closed-weight reference)
models.key_results: Table V (P strategy): F1_micro 0.96, DOC_micro 0.52, Score 0.74 -- tied for #1 with Gemma3-27B; parameter count listed as '1.8T*' with footnote 'This is an approximation. No valid information available.' Excluded from size-based/combined open-model analyses since parameters/training data undisclosed (Sec. V-A). Table VII: N_err 473 (P) / 555 (PJ+); WV dominates at 96.4% (P) and 98.0% (PJ+). Sec. VII: 'GPT-4o was added as a closed-weight reference, it did not exhibit a clear advantage over the best open-source models (e.g. Gemma3-27B)'.

headline_numbers: Dataset: 995 total test cases across 5 use cases, 199 validated cases each (Table I; Sec. III).

Prompting-strategy aggregate outcomes across all models/tests: P = 7,791 correct / 14,171 mistakes / 2,879 errors / 1,597 failed; PJ+ = 7,646 correct / 20,652 mistakes / 1,169 errors / 18 failed (Table III).
Strategy win-count across 22 models: P wins for 11 models, PJ+ for 8, PJ for 3, J and J+ for 0 (Table IV).

Top leaderboard scores (P strategy, Table V): GPT-4o and Gemma3-27B tie at F1_micro 0.96 / DOC_micro 0.52 / composite Score 0.74 (highest); worst is Phi3-3.8B at F1_micro 0.95 / DOC_micro 0.01 / Score 0.48.

F1_micro (value/token-level accuracy) is fairly flat and high across all models/sizes (0.89-0.96, Table V) and scales log-linearly with parameter count (Fig. 8); DOC_micro (document-level full validity) is far more volatile (0.01-0.52) and depends much more on prompting-strategy/model-family fit than on scale (Sec. VI-B, Fig. 8-9, Sec. VII).

WV (Wrong Value) errors are the dominant, persistent failure across essentially all configurations and model sizes -- for Deepseek-R1 under P, WV incidence rises from 76.7% (1.5B) to 97-98% (14B-70B) of erroneous cases, and under PJ+ reaches 99.7-100% (Table VI); GPT-4o and Gemma3-27B (top performers) also show 96.4-100% WV incidence among their errors (Table VII).
failure_modes: Recursive GT-comparison classifies errors into Missing Key (MK, key absent), Missing Value (MV, value null/absent), and Wrong Value (WV, value present but incorrect -- subdivided into value deviation via Levenshtein distance, value error, coercible type mismatch, and hard type error) (Sec. IV). Document-level failures (complete parse failure, markdown-wrapped output, fatal schema violation) are captured separately via DOC_micro (Sec. IV-A). Key trends: (1) MK/schema-violation failures are concentrated in small models under weak prompting (e.g., Deepseek-R1-1.5B under P: 36.8% of errors are MK) and essentially vanish by ~7-8B parameters or under stronger schema-enforcing prompts (PJ+ drives MK to 0.0% for Deepseek-R1 at every size, Table VI). (2) Once structural failures are eliminated, WV becomes the dominant and persistent bottleneck at every scale, including the largest/best models (GPT-4o, Gemma3-27B) -- 'WV errors remain the primary bottleneck across all configurations' (Sec. VII). (3) Prompting-strategy choice can trade one failure mode for another rather than eliminating it: switching Deepseek-R1 from P to PJ+ eliminates schema failures/MK almost entirely but 'redirects almost every remaining uncertainty into field content', converting some previously-perfect parses into WV cases (e.g., 433 perfect runs at 14B under P shrink to 364 under PJ+) (Sec. VI-C). (4) Some model families fail catastrophically under specific strategies -- the Phi family (esp. Phi3-3.8B, Phi3.5-3.8B) performs very poorly under P (DOC_micro as low as 0.01-0.03) but recovers under PJ+ (Sec. VI-A, Table V, Table VIII). (5) DOC_micro variance increases with model size for several families (Deepseek-R1, Qwen3, Gemma3), attributed to prompt-model compatibility rather than scale (Sec. VI-B, Fig. 6-7).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The entire benchmark is single-shot / one-forward-pass per test case: there is no iterative refinement, no multi-turn repair, no interaction loop, and no chain-of-thought or agentic strategy studied (Sec. III 'Benchmark setup': 'It is instructed to return a single JSON object adhering to this schema'). The paper's central lesson operates entirely within this single-shot regime: 'choosing the right prompting strategy is more important than standard attributes such as model size' (Abstract), and 'prompting strategy and model architecture are at least as important as model scale for JSON parsing tasks' (Sec. VII). The paper does draw a one-shot vs. more-demonstration-adjacent distinction only in the sense that its five prompting strategies vary how much schema/example guidance is embedded in a single prompt (API format param alone vs. full in-prompt schema + one example pair vs. both) -- but even the richest strategy (PJ+) is still exactly one example pair and one forward call, not few-shot with multiple examples or a multi-turn dialogue. The paper offers no data on what changes if models get multiple attempts, feedback, or repair loops; that comparison is out of scope entirely.

relation_to_candidate.position: Family-axis position: single-shot only (one call, one example pair, schema in-context) -- no interactive/multi-turn variant is studied. What is observed: a full JSON object extracted from a natural-language message against a provided schema (semantic content extraction, not pure serialization-convention compliance). Output shape: a full nested JSON structure (up to depth 4, arrays of objects) -- structurally much richer than c11's single canonical string, but scored non-exactly (graded F1_micro over key/value diffs plus a separate document-validity term), not by whole-string exact match. Verifier type: a custom recursive structural/semantic diff classifier (MK/MV/WV taxonomy) feeding two composable graded metrics (F1_micro, DOC_micro), rather than c11's single deterministic 0/1 whole-string-exact-match against a canonicalizer reference function.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c11: Both are JSON-centric structured-output tasks scored against a schema/reference, both study format-compliance vs. content-correctness as a live tension, and both are explicitly framed as complementary to JSONSchemaBench (Geng et al.) on schema-constrained decoding -- LLMStructBench cites it directly (Sec. II) as does c11's own lineage notes (which list JSONSchemaBench as 'the schema-pool donor' for calibrating canonicalization difficulty). Both surface a recurring finding relevant to c11's design concerns: schema/structural compliance (MK, DOC_micro) is comparatively easy to fix via prompting and saturates quickly with scale, while fine-grained value/content correctness (WV, c11's exact-string diff) is the harder, more persistent bottleneck that does not go away with scale -- directly supporting c11's design premise that per-rule strata (analogous to WV subtypes) are where discriminative signal lives.
relation_to_candidate.differences: LLMStructBench extracts information from free-text into a schema-conformant JSON object (an information-extraction/semantic-fidelity task); c11 canonicalizes an already-well-specified record/string into one exact target string under invented, non-standard formatting rules (a pure normalization/rule-following task with no extraction ambiguity). LLMStructBench's ground truth is inherently graded/fuzzy-matchable (Levenshtein similarity, type coercion credit) because natural-language paraphrase creates legitimate near-matches; c11 is strict 0/1 whole-string exact match because there is exactly one correct canonical serialization and no paraphrase tolerance is appropriate. LLMStructBench varies prompting strategy and model scale as its independent variables and holds the target JSON schema/convention fixed (standard JSON, no invented house rules); c11 holds the prompting essentially fixed and instead varies which invented house rules must be inferred/applied, with per-rule strata as the discriminative axis. LLMStructBench is entirely single-shot with one example pair; c11's design documents (per c11.md) discuss demo-set/GEPA-minibatch coverage risk across optimization iterations, an axis LLMStructBench does not study at all (it has no optimization loop).
relation_to_candidate.what_c11_would_add: c11 would add: (1) a strict, unambiguous exact-match verifier that isolates rule-following fidelity from semantic/extraction ambiguity -- LLMStructBench's fuzzy value-matching (Levenshtein credit, coercible-mismatch partial credit) cannot detect a single-character formatting slip the way c11's whole-string match can; (2) per-rule attribution (each house rule is an independently scoreable stratum, diff-visible), versus LLMStructBench's coarser MK/MV/WV taxonomy which does not decompose by 'which specific convention rule was violated'; (3) contamination-proofing via invented (not merely under-documented) conventions -- LLMStructBench's schemas are realistic and could resemble seen data, whereas c11's house rules are deliberately non-standard and infinitely regenerable; (4) explicit study of the demo-coverage/optimization-leakage risk (GEPA minibatch vs. rule-table width) that has no analogue in LLMStructBench's static single-shot design.

corpus_claim: This paper was cited only as a bare arXiv id (2602.14743) in c11's notes with no title; its identity was verified during acquisition (see the file header). Assess what it actually is and whether/how it is relevant to c11.
corpus_claim_verdict: miscited (as framed by the task) / not actually cited on c11's page. The paper's identity is genuinely and correctly resolved -- it is real: 'LLMStructBench: Benchmarking Large Language Model Structured Data Extraction' by Tenckhoff, Koddenbrock, and Rodner (HTW Berlin), arXiv:2602.14743, v1 submitted 16 Feb 2026 (paper header lines 2-10; matches the file's own discrepancy note). However, c11.md's own text does NOT cite this paper anywhere in its body -- the sole mention is a closing provenance-flag footnote: 'Note on provenance flags: LLMStructBench (arXiv:2602.14743) and siblings DTBench/ExtractBench are provenance-unverified, future-dated corpus items attributed to c12, not c11 -- none are cited on this page' (c11.md, final line). c12.md corroborates: it lists 'DTBench / ExtractBench / LLMStructBench (literature, motivation-only)' as 'provenance-unverified; no canonical venue page could be located for any of the three... Treat all three as unverifiable placeholders, not citations' (c12.md line 57). So the premise that this paper was 'cited in c11's notes' does not hold within the current corpus files: it belongs (as an unverified, motivation-only placeholder) to c12's lineage discussion, not c11's. Substantively, though, the paper IS topically adjacent to c11 (JSON structured-output benchmarking, format-compliance vs. semantic-correctness tension) and, per the analysis above, is genuinely more relevant to c12 (Sigil-Schema Field Extraction, which is about schema-conformant extraction) than to c11 (canonicalization under invented house rules), since LLMStructBench studies extraction accuracy/schema validity, not canonical-serialization exactness under deviating conventions.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials used: 5 use cases spanning JSON depth 2-4, with 5-10 top-level keys and 5-9 leaf values, one use case (Loan request) using arrays of nested objects (Table I) -- useful as a model for how c11 might scale its own record complexity/nesting for baseline difficulty tiers.

Prompt-format axis that swung accuracy hardest: NOT the schema depth, but how JSON output was enforced -- API-level format parameter ("json" string vs. full JSON-Schema object) crossed with in-prompt schema+example guidance produced swings from 18 to 1,597 total failures across the model pool (Table III) and DOC_micro swings from 0.01 to 0.52 for the same or similar models depending on strategy (Table V, Table VIII) -- strong evidence that prompt/output-mode framing, not just task difficulty, can dominate measured accuracy, a confound c11's baseline design should control for explicitly.

Metrics: F1_micro (fuzzy, coercion- and Levenshtein-credit-weighted micro-F1 over key:value pairs, alpha=0.25 key/value split) and DOC_micro (document-level full-correctness share) combined with lambda=0.5 into one composite score (Sec. IV-A, IV-B) -- both graded/partial-credit metrics, contrasting with c11's strict 0/1 exact-match; useful negative example showing why c11's designers chose whole-string exact match to avoid masking rule-violation signal.

Floors/ceilings observed: F1_micro floor ~0.89 (Gemma3-1B) to ceiling 0.96 (GPT-4o, Gemma3-27B) -- a narrow, high-floor range showing value-level token accuracy saturates quickly and doesn't discriminate models well; DOC_micro floor 0.01 (Phi3-3.8B, P strategy) to ceiling 0.52 (top models) -- a much wider, more discriminative range (Table V). This suggests document/structural-validity-style all-or-nothing metrics (closer to c11's exact-match) are far more discriminative between models than fuzzy token metrics, supporting c11's choice of strict scoring.

Contamination/data-release status: dataset is described as 'open' and synthetically generated via GPT-4o then manually verified/cleaned (Sec. III-A, III-B) -- synthetic-then-verified generation is explicitly offered as a route to diverse, schema-valid natural test cases, analogous to c11's own synthetic-instance-generation plan (though c11 additionally needs contamination-proofing via invented, not just synthetic, conventions).

Complexity ceiling/generation failure note directly relevant to designing harder c11 strata: 'We also experimented with use cases and schema that involved more nested JSON-objects, however, this did not result in even medium quality generated examples and have been therefore skipped. Often times the generation of the email was missing out on many crucial values from the ground-truth base object' (Sec. III-C) -- a caution that increasing structural complexity in synthetic generation pipelines can silently degrade instance quality, relevant to c11's own generator-quality risk when scaling rule-table width or record nesting.
quotes: "Each model receives a natural-language message, a representative example pair (text and reference JSON), and the corresponding JSON schema that defines the required structure. It is instructed to return a single JSON object adhering to this schema." (Sec. III, 'Benchmark setup')
"In particular, we show that choosing the right prompting strategy is more important than standard attributes such as model size." (Abstract)
"WV errors remain the primary bottleneck across all configurations, suggesting that post-generation semantic validation or fine-tuning is needed for further gains." (Sec. VII, Conclusion)

"PJ+ strategy is the safest choice for ensuring parseable outputs, especially for small or structurally unreliable models, but this comes at the cost of increased semantic errors." (Sec. VII, Conclusion)
"Model size helps, particularly for improving value accuracy (F1_micro), but DOC_micro is more sensitive to prompt-model compatibility than to scale." (Sec. VII, Conclusion)

"We also experimented with use cases and schema that involved more nested JSON-objects, however, this did not result in even medium quality generated examples and have been therefore skipped." (Sec. III-C)

"Note on provenance flags: LLMStructBench (arXiv:2602.14743) and siblings DTBench/ExtractBench are provenance-unverified, future-dated corpus items attributed to c12, not c11 -- none are cited on this page." (c11.md, closing note)

"DTBench / ExtractBench / LLMStructBench (literature, motivation-only) -- listed in the merge as motivation but provenance-unverified; no canonical venue page could be located for any of the three... Treat all three as unverifiable placeholders, not citations." (c12.md, line 57)

corrections: models[Phi family].key_results: dossier had attached '(worst DOC_micro of any model under P)' to Phi3.5-3.8B (DOC_micro 0.03). Table V shows Phi3-3.8B has DOC_micro 0.01, which is actually the lowest in the table (lower than Phi3.5-3.8B's 0.03). Moved the 'worst DOC_micro' qualifier to the Phi3-3.8B entry (merged with the pre-existing, correct 'lowest overall score' note) and removed the incorrect parenthetical from Phi3.5-3.8B.
